%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.7-3 Vector Operations

> **Objective:** Master MATLAB vector creation syntax, endpoint inclusion rules, and 1-based indexing — the three patterns most likely to appear on exams.

Here is the key example to anchor everything: `k = 0:2:11` gives `[0 2 4 6 8 10]`. The format is **start:step:end**. Memorize four rules cold: (1) `a:b:c` means start at \(a\), step by \(b\), stop at or before \(c\); (2) if the step is omitted, MATLAB defaults to 1; (3) the endpoint is included **only** if the stepping lands on it exactly; (4) MATLAB indexing starts at 1, not 0. Vectorization means one expression can compute many values simultaneously — feed a vector into a formula and get a vector back. This section is tested through output prediction, not derivation.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Three canonical forms of MATLAB vector creation. Note that \(11:-10/3:0\) does not include 0 because the final step overshoots it.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215210977-6829.png)

## 1. Creating Vectors Fast

The general syntax is `a:b:c`, where \(a\) is the **initial value**, \(b\) is the **step size**, and \(c\) is the **termination value**. MATLAB generates a row vector by repeatedly adding \(b\) to \(a\) until the next value would exceed \(c\) (or fall below \(c\) for negative steps).

### THREE TESTABLE CASES

| Form | Step | Example | Result |
|------|------|---------|--------|
| `a:b:c` | Positive | `0:2:11` | `[0 2 4 6 8 10]` |
| `a:b:c` | Negative | `11:-10/3:0` | `[11 7.6667 4.3333 1.0000]` |
| `a:c` | Omitted (=1) | `0:11` | `[0 1 2 ... 11]` |

### FAST RECOGNITION RULE

Predict the next terms mentally and stop the moment the next value would cross the end. For `0:2:11`: the sequence is \(0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10\) — the next would be \(12\), which crosses \(11\), so stop at \(10\). The value \(11\) is never reached.

This is usually tested as output prediction.

$$w = e^{j\left(\pi/3 + 2\pi k/3\right)}$$
*By feeding a vector \(k = [0, 1, 2]\) into this formula, MATLAB computes all three cube roots of \(-1\) in a single command instead of evaluating them one by one.*

## 2. Vectorization and Indexing Traps

MATLAB becomes powerful when a scalar formula receives a vector input — one expression returns many outputs simultaneously. The cube-root formula above is a direct example: define `k = 0:2` and MATLAB evaluates \(w\) at \(k = 0, 1, 2\) all at once.

### THE INDEXING TRAP

This is the most common exam mistake. **MATLAB indices start at 1**, but the mathematical variable \(k\) may start at 0 or any other value. Index position and variable value are **not** the same thing.

> **Critical contrast:** If `k = 0:99`, then `w(5)` is the **fifth stored value**, which corresponds to \(k = 4\), not \(k = 5\). Similarly, for a sampled sinusoid stored in vector `f`, `f(1)` means the **first stored sample** — it does not mean the function evaluated at \(t = 1\).

### EXAM TIP

Whenever you see vector indexing on an exam, ask two separate questions: (1) what is the index position? (2) what is the actual variable value at that position? They are almost never equal unless the variable also starts at 1.

Always distinguish storage position from mathematical input variable.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="trap_exposure" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Left: `k(5)` retrieves the fifth stored value, which is \(k = 4\). Right: `f(1)` is the first stored sample, corresponding to \(t = 0\), not \(t = 1\).*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215390515-7090.png)

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- `a:b:c` means start at \(a\), step by \(b\), stop at or before \(c\); omit \(b\) for step = 1.
- The endpoint is included only when repeated stepping lands on it exactly — otherwise it is skipped.
- MATLAB indices start at 1; index position and the mathematical variable value are usually different.

*In the next section we will visualize vectors and sampled signals with plotting.*
